segments.s396-g81: شما گفت: here 'شما' is the 2nd-person plural/formal 'you'. 'گفت' is a past-tense/say verb used in narrative/quoted speech ('you said' / 'you claim'). The prepositional phrase 'از ایران به پند آمدید' has the pattern 'از X به Y آمدن' (came from X to Y) where 'از ایران' = 'from Iran', 'به پند' = 'for/to give advice' and 'آمدید' is the 2nd-person plural past 'you came'.
segments.s396-g82: نه ... آمدید negates the preceding purpose: 'نه از بهر نام بلند آمدید' = 'you did not come for the sake of high name'. 'از بهر' is a fixed idiom meaning 'for the sake of / because of', 'نام بلند' = 'lofty name' (i.e. great fame), and 'آمدید' repeats the past-tense 'you came' to contrast purpose.
segments.s396-g83: 'کجا' begins a rhetorical question 'Where are...'. 'آن همه خلعت و پند شاه' lists things being asked about: 'آن همه' = 'all those', 'خلعت' = 'robes of honour' (ceremonial gifts), 'و پند شاه' = 'and the king's counsel' (pind = advice). Word order is noun phrases stacked without an explicit verb (typical in poetry; the verb is implied in the question).
segments.s396-g84: This segment continues the list of items: 'کمرهای زرین' = 'golden belts/girdles' ('کمرهای' = plural of 'کمر' used as 'girdles'), followed by 'و تخت و کلاه' = 'and the throne and caps/headsgear'. The nouns are coordinated with 'و' and no copula is present (poetic nominal list).
segments.s396-g85: Again a list introduced by 'کجا آن همه' ('Where are all those...'). 'عهد و سوگند و بند' enumerates 'pledge/covenant (عهد)', 'oath (سوگند)', and 'bond/tie (بند)'. The series emphasizes past solemn obligations; no finite verb appears because the clause is elliptical/rhetorical.
segments.s396-g86: 'به یزدان' attaches the preceding religious pledges to 'God' ('to God' / 'before God'). The following 'و آن اختر سودمند' adds 'and that beneficial/auspicious star' — 'سودمند' here qualifies 'اختر' as favorable. The whole is still part of the rhetorical question (no explicit verb).
segments.s396-g87: 'که' introduces an explanatory/cause clause ('for/that'), 'اکنون' = 'now', 'چنین سست شد پایتان' = 'your feet/footing became thus weak'. 'شد' is the past of 'شدن' (become), and 'پایتان' ('your feet' / 'your step') is the possessed noun standing as the affected element.
segments.s396-g88: 'به ره' = 'on the way / along the road', 'بر پراگنده شد رایتان' = 'your banners were scattered upon/over it'. Here 'بر' = 'upon', 'پراگنده شد' is a passive/intransitive perfective sense 'were scattered' (literally 'became scattered'), and 'رایتان' names the thing scattered ('your banners/standards'—poetic possessive).
segments.s396-g33: This phrase sets a conditional: subject first ('همه ریگ' = all/the whole sand) followed by the predicative 'تفته است' (is lumped/compacted). The enclitic-like poetic form 'است‌گر' functions as an archaic conditional 'if it is', and the following 'خاک و شخ' names 'the earth and the (onomatopoeic) shakh' as things being described. Note the poetic inversion and the fused conditional 'است‌گر' rather than a modern separate 'اگر...است'.
segments.s396-g34: This is the main clause after the conditional: the prepositional phrase 'بر او' (on it/over it) is followed by the negative verb 'نگذرد' (does not pass), and the series of nouns 'مرغ و مور و ملخ' (bird and ant and locust) is placed after the verb — a common poetic verb‑first order. The meaning is ‘no bird, ant, or locust passes over it.’
segments.s396-g35: 'نبینی' is a negative present (poetic) form meaning 'you will not / one does not see'. It is followed by the locative 'به جایی' ('in any place' / 'anywhere') and the quantified object 'یکی قطره آب' ('a single drop of water'). The verb precedes its complements, which gives the line a terse, emphatic tone.
segments.s396-g36: 'زمینش' ('its earth/ground') is the subject; 'همی' is an older continuous/emphatic marker meaning 'still/keeps' or 'continually'; 'جوشد' is a present tense verb 'boils/seethes'. The phrase 'از آفتاب' gives the cause ('from/because of the sun'). Together: 'its ground continually boils from the sun.'
segments.s396-g37: This clause begins with the negative 'نه' (nor/not), then the locative 'بر خاک او' ('on its soil'), followed by the noun 'شیر' (lion) and the verb phrase 'یابد گذر' ('finds passage' / 'finds a way to pass'). The order is verb-final in sense but poetic: literally 'not on its soil does a lion find passage.'
segments.s396-g38: The correlative negative 'نه' starts the second clause ('nor...'), 'اندر هوا' means 'in the air', and 'کرگس تیزپر' is a nominal phrase 'the swift‑winged vulture'. The verb is omitted (a common poetic ellipsis), so the clause means 'nor (is there) a swift‑winged vulture in the air.'
segments.s396-g39: Starting with the negative 'نه' ('no' / 'not'), the locative 'بر شخ و ریگش' ('on the shakh and its sand') is followed by the verb phrase 'بروید گیا' — a poetic order where the verb 'بروید' (from 'روئیدن', to grow / to appear) comes before the noun 'گیا' (plants). The clause therefore reads 'no plant grows on the shakh and its sand.'
segments.s396-g40: 'زمینش' ('its ground') is followed by the descriptive phrase 'روان ریگ' where 'روان' functions as an adjective 'flowing' or 'running' modifying 'ریگ' (sand). 'چون توتیا' means 'like white lead' (a simile). The whole verbless line says 'its ground's flowing sand is like white lead.'
segments.s396-g57: چو is a temporal subordinating particle meaning "when" introducing the clause. ایرانیان is the subject (plural) after چو as is normal in Persian narrative word order. این بد is a nominal phrase "this ill/evil/news," and از گرگسار is a prepositional phrase (from the wolf-haunt) marking the source of the news.
segments.s396-g58: شنیدند is the simple past (3rd person plural) "they heard/learned." و links the two coordinated predicates. گشتند here is past (3rd pl.) of گشتن with the meaning "became/turned (into a state)" — i.e. they became afflicted. با introduces the cause or accompaniment, درد یار means "grief/sorrow for (their) companion" (Persian often omits the ezāfe or uses juxtaposition in poetry).
segments.s396-g59: بگفتند is a reporting verb in past tense (they said). کای is a vocative particle "O," addressing the addressee; شاه آزادمرد is the vocative phrase "king, noble man" (آزادمرد here an epithet). The verb precedes the vocative phrase, a common epic inversion for direct speech.
segments.s396-g60: به گرد بلا is a prepositional phrase meaning roughly "toward/into the midst of calamity" (به + گرد, گرد = "around/about/the midst of", بلا = "calamity"). تا توانی is a subordinate temporal/conditional phrase "as long as you can" (تا = "until/as long as", توانی = 2nd‑person present/subjunctive "you can"). مگرد is an imperative/negative form meaning "do not turn/return/go" addressed to the king (archaic/poetic negative imperative); its precise sense is not among the provided sense options for this token.
segments.s396-g61: اگر introduces a conditional clause "if." گرگسار (literally "wolf-haunt") names the speaker or source in this conditional. این سخنها is the direct object/topic "these words," and که گفت is a relative/clause marker + verb indicating "that (he) said" — the clause modifies سخنها. The overall chunk means "if the wolf-haunt said these words / if what the wolf‑lair said..." Note: the verb گفت functions here as the past tense "said," a reading not given in the supplied sense list (so its sense index is absent).
segments.s396-g62: چنین است is a copular expression "it is thus/so." این خود (literally "this itself") emphasizes the matter under discussion. نماند is the present/future‑oriented form of ماندن in narration meaning "will not remain/last/be left," and نهفت is a past participle/adjective "hidden/concealed." Together نماند نهفت = "will not remain hidden." The clause states that, if the foe's words are true, the matter will not stay concealed.
segments.s396-g63: بدین جایگه is an archaic/poetic prepositional phrase "to this place/spot" (بدین = به + این). مرگ را places مرگ (death) with the object/topic marker را — in this idiomatic order the phrase means "we have come to meet (or for) death." آمدیم is the first‑person plural past "we came/arrived." The word order (object before verb) is a rhetorical inversion common in epic poetry.
segments.s396-g64: نه introduces contrast/negation "not/it is not that." فرسودن is the infinitival/verbal noun "to wear out/consume by wear." ترگ (rag) is the direct object of فرسودن and is marked again by را; آمدیم (we came) repeats the past tense verb, giving a parallel antithesis: "we came for death, not to wear out rags." The construction uses infinitival noun + object + آمدیم to state purpose/contrast.
segments.s396-g65: چنین راه دشوار بگذاشتی is a clause in which the demonstrative چنین ('such/so') modifies راه دشوار ('a difficult way/path'). بگذاشتی is the past (2nd‑person singular) form with the prefixed b‑ (بـ+) found in classical/poetic Persian, meaning 'you placed/set' (i.e., 'you made/laid down'). The noun phrase precedes the verb, a normal word order in Persian where the verb comes at the end.
segments.s396-g66: بلای دد و دام برداشتی: بلای ('the calamity/scourge') is the direct object, followed by a coordinated noun phrase دد و دام ('enemy(s) and trap(s)'). برداشتی is again past 2nd‑person singular of برداشتن with the prefix omitted in the lemma list; here it means 'you took up' or 'you brought upon (us)'. The object comes before the verb, and the verb carries the past tense marking for 'you'.
segments.s396-g67: کس از نامداران و شاهان گرد is a nominal phrase meaning 'no one among the famed and the kings.' Here کس is used in the negative-sense 'no one.' از marks the group 'among/of,' نامداران و شاهان is the coordinated noun phrase, and گرد here functions adverbially as 'around/among' (i.e., 'among them').
segments.s396-g68: چنین رنجها برنیارد شمرد is the predicate: چنین رنجها ('such hardships') is the object/topic and شمرد is the past 3rd‑person (or a perfective past) of 'to count/reckon.' The intervening form برنیارد is not matched by the provided senses (it appears to be an archaic/compressed form or a mis-tokenization) and functions here as part of the verbal expression; because it does not match the listed senses, its sense is marked unknown. Overall the clause says that no one among the famous kings had reckoned/experienced such sufferings.
segments.s396-g69: که پیش تو آمد بدین هفتخوان is a subordinate temporal clause introduced by که used here in the sense 'when/that.' پیش تو آمد literally 'comes/comes before you' (or 'when you come before'), with آمد in a narrative/past form used to indicate the time of the action. بدین is the fused prepositional phrase 'ب + این' meaning 'to/into these' and هفتخوان is the compound meaning 'seven trials/tests.' The clause functions as 'when you arrive at these seven trials.'
segments.s396-g70: بر این بر جهان‌آفرین را بخوان is an imperative instruction: بر این ('for this' / 'on this matter') followed by the object جهان‌آفرین ('the world‑creator' = God) marked by را (the definite/object marker), and بخوان is the 2nd‑person singular imperative or present‑subjunctive of خواندن meaning 'recite/call (upon) / pray.' The two occurrences of بر function as 'on/for' in a poetic repetition; the verb بخوان issues the command 'call/recite.'
segments.s396-g71: چو پیروزگر بازگردی به راه: چو is a temporal/conditional conjunction meaning 'when' or 'if.' پیروزگر ('victor' / 'triumphant') qualifies the subject; بازگردی is the present/subjunctive or jussive form used in protasis ('when you return' / 'if you return'), addressed to 'you.' به راه is a locative phrase 'on the road / on your way.'
segments.s396-g72: به دل شاد و خرم شوی نزد شاه: به دل شاد و خرم is a circumstantial phrase 'with a happy and joyful heart.' شوی is the present/subjunctive (or stative 'become') of شدن used here to mean 'be' or 'become' (addressing 'you'); نزد شاه means 'in the presence of the king' and is placed after the verb as the final locative phrase. The clause as a whole is an exhortation: be joyful before the king.
segments.s396-g113: ز ما here means 'from us' or 'among us' (poetic preposition + pronoun). تا بود is a temporal clause 'as long as / until there was' (تا = 'as long as', بود = past 'was'). زنده نامدار is a noun phrase with the adjective following the noun: 'a renowned (نامدار) one who is alive (زنده)'.
segments.s396-g114: نپیچیم is negative (ن-) + پیچیم (1st-person-plural form) and here means 'we do not turn away / we will not retreat' (negative optative/indicative sense). یک تن is an emphatic measure phrase 'even a single person' (تن = person/body). سر از کارزار is an idiom 'head from the battle' = 'to turn away from the fighting' (سر = 'head', از = 'from', کارزار = 'battle').
segments.s396-g115: سپهبد is the subject (the military commander). چو بشنید = 'when he heard' (چو = 'when', بشنید = past 'he heard'). ز ایشان سخن = 'speech/words from them' (ز = 'from', ایشان = 'them', سخن = 'speech/words').
segments.s396-g116: بپیچید is a past action of پیچیدن used idiomatically here meaning 'he turned to / he answered / he uttered' (the verb 'to turn' used as 'to respond/recite'). زان is 'from/with that' (referring to what follows). گفته‌های کهن = 'the old/ancient sayings' (گفته‌های plural 'sayings/statements', کهن = 'ancient').
segments.s396-g117: به ایرانیان آفرین کرد = 'he praised the Iranians' (به = 'to/for', ایرانیان = 'Iranians', آفرین = 'acclamation/praise', کرد = past 'he did/made' → 'he gave praise'). و گفت = 'and said' (و = 'and', گفت = past 'said').
segments.s396-g118: که introduces reported speech 'that'. هرگز نماند = 'never remains / will never be left' (هرگز = 'never', نماند = negative/optative or future sense of 'remain'). هنر در نهفت = 'skill/virtue (هنر) hidden (نهفت) in/within (در)' → 'skill would not remain hidden'.
segments.s396-g119: گر = 'if' (conditional). ایدون here has an adverbial/temporal force 'now / from now on' (classical usage; literal sense 'come/appear'). که گردیم = 'that we become' (که linking particle + گردیم, subjunctive/optative of گشتن 'to become'). پیروزگر = 'triumphant / victorious' describing the result.
segments.s396-g120: ز رنج گذشته = 'from/out of past suffering' (ز = 'from', رنج = 'suffering/hardship', گذشته = 'past'). بیابیم is a subjunctive/optative form of یافتن 'find/attain' here meaning 'let us find/let us recover' (so the grammatical sense is subjunctive). بر after بیابیم conveys the sense 'over / in exchange / against' (i.e. to obtain something over/against the past hardship).
segments.s396-g185: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning "when" introducing a subordinate temporal clause. خور is the subject meaning "the sun." چادر زرد بر سرکشید is a verbal phrase in which بر + سرکشید describes the action of casting or drawing something over the head (literally "lifted/reared over the head"); this poetic compound describes the sun as if putting a yellow cloak/veil on the sky. The verb is past tense (perfective) describing a completed action.
segments.s396-g186: ببد is a past/stative verb meaning "became/turned." باختر is the locative/time/compass noun "the west" (i.e. the western sky at sunset). چون functions as a simile marker "like/as." گل شنبلید is the comparison term "a [hyacinth/fenugreek] flower," so the whole segment says the west "became like a flower."
segments.s396-g187: بنه here denotes a small bundle/heap used idiomatically for an encampment or the act of making camp; برنهادند is a past plural transitive verb "they raised/put up" (they pitched). گردان همه places the noun "troop/unit" before the quantifier "all/everyone," so together it means the entire troop took action (they all set up camp).
segments.s396-g188: برفتند is a past plural intransitive verb "they departed/they went away." با is the preposition "with," شهریار is the noun "king," and رمه here means a large crowd/host rather than literally a herd; the phrase says that the forces departed accompanying the king.
segments.s396-g189: چو introduces a temporal clause "when." بگذشت is a past-tense verb "passed." از تیره شب literally "from the dark night" modifies the passing, and یک زمان is a temporal adverbial "for a moment/for an instant," so the clause situates a brief moment after the dark night passed.
segments.s396-g190: خروش is a noun "a roar/clamor." کلنگ here is an image likening the sound to that of a pickaxe (a poetic metaphor). آمد is the past verb "came," and از آسمان "from the sky" gives the source; the whole noun phrase indicates a sudden loud sound issuing from the sky.
segments.s396-g191: برآشفت is a past/stative verb meaning "he became upset/was agitated." ز is the poetic variant of از meaning "from/because of," linking the agitation to آوازش "its sound/voice" (آواز + possessive -ش). اسفندیار is the subject name placed after the clause for emphasis.
segments.s396-g192: پیامی is an indefinite accusative/oblique noun "a message." فرستاد is the past transitive verb "(he) sent." زی is an archaic/poetic preposition here used in the sense related to "from/of/to" (classical usage varies), linking the verb to گرگسار, a noun meaning "wolf-infested place/ferocious (ones)." The whole phrase means he dispatched a message concerning or directed to the wolf-haunted quarter.
segments.s396-g17: This noun phrase establishes the main topic: یکی کار پیش است فردا = 'there is a certain matter ahead tomorrow'. یکی + کار = 'a certain matter'; پیش است (lit. 'is before/ahead') marks imminence. که is the relative/complementizer introducing the following clause (it links this noun phrase to what that matter is).
segments.s396-g18: This completes the relative clause introduced by که: مرد نیندیشد از روزگار نبرد = 'a man would not think/imagine (that) from fate comes a battle.' نیندیشد is a negative present/generic (narrative) verb meaning 'would not think / does not imagine'; از + روزگار gives the source/agent 'from fate'; نبرد is the object 'battle'.
segments.s396-g19: A correlative negative: نه ... نه ... with the verb یادت آید following the first coordinated objects. گرز و کمان = 'mace and bow'; یادت آید (literally 'comes to your memory') functions as 'you will recall / it will occur to you'; تیغ is appended in the final negative 'nor sword'. The verb after the first items governs both negatives.
segments.s396-g20: Continues the double negation: نه بیند ... = 'nor does he see ...'. بیند is the present/generic of 'see'; ره جنگ و راه گریغ are two parallel noun phrases 'the road/route of battle' and 'the way/path of (pride/aloofness)'; the initial نه negates the verb and thus both coordinated objects.
segments.s396-g21: Locative expression + clause: به بالای یک نیزه = 'on the top of a spear' (به + بالای = 'on/at the top of'); برف آیدت = 'snow will come to you / fall upon you', where آیدت is آید + enclitic ت 'to you' (verbal predicate indicating the event befalls the addressee).
segments.s396-g22: بدو is a demonstrative/locative 'to that / to it' (pointing back to the situation just described). روز شادی شگرف is a noun phrase 'a marvelous day of joy', and آیدت repeats the verb 'will come to you' (آید + ت). The whole line: 'to that (event) a wondrous day of joy will come upon you.'
segments.s396-g23: بمانی تو = 'you will remain' (verb before pronoun is a common poetic inversion). با لشکر نامدار = 'with the renowned/illustrious army' (با + noun phrase). The verb bماندی expresses continued state; the whole clause forecasts or states that 'you remain/shall remain with the famed host.'
segments.s396-g24: به برف اندر‌ای combines locative elements: به + برف + اندر = 'in/into the snow'. اندر‌ای here includes the vocative particle ای appended to اندر (effectively 'in the snow, O ...'), so the following فرخ اسفندیار is a vocative: 'O fortunate Esfandiyar.' The segment therefore reads 'in the snow, O fortunate Esfandiyar.'
segments.s396-g49: This is an SOV/verb‑final clause in literary Persian: the preposition 'ز' (from) begins the phrase 'ز بیرون' (from outside), followed by the negative/stative verb 'نیابد' (does not find/obtain). 'خورش' here functions as 'its food/what it eats' and 'چارپای' (four‑footed creature) is the object of that need. The verb 'نیابد' is third‑person singular present (classical style) meaning 'does not/ cannot find.'
segments.s396-g50: Another independent clause: 'ز لشکر' (from the army/host) + verb 'نماند' (is not left / remains not) + 'سواری به جای' (a rider/horseman in place). Word order places the prepositional source first; 'نماند' is third‑person singular perfective/present meaning 'is left/ remains.' The fixed expression 'به جای' means 'in (his) place' or 'available as replacement.'
segments.s396-g51: 'از ایران و توران' = 'from Iran and Turan.' The conditional particle 'اگر' ('if') introduces the hypothetical quantity 'صدهزار' (one hundred thousand). Word order is topical (place/source first) then condition and number.
segments.s396-g52: The verb 'بیایند' is third‑person plural present/subjunctive ('they come' / 'may come') used here in the protasis/apodosis pattern; 'گردان' (troop/unit) and 'خنجرگزار' (literally 'dagger‑bearer' used as an epithet) form the noun phrase complement: '(there will) come a troop of dagger‑bearers.'
segments.s396-g53: Literal word order: verb 'نشینند' (they sit / they take up position) + duration 'صد سال' (a hundred years) + adverbial 'گرد اندرش' (around it). 'اندرش' = 'in/around it' (اندر + ـش = 'in/on it'). The verb is third‑person plural present/habitual describing prolonged action.
segments.s396-g54: 'همی' is the continuous/adverbial prefix meaning 'continually' or 'still'; 'تیرباران کنند' is a compound verbal phrase 'to make a shower of arrows' (کنند = they do/make). 'از برش' literally 'from/at its side' — here indicating the source/target of the arrowing. The whole segment describes an ongoing action: they continually shower it with arrows.
segments.s396-g55: 'فراوان همانست' = 'abundant/many, it is the same' — the copula 'ست' (is) attaches to 'همان' (the same/that). The conjunction 'و' links the next clause 'کمتر همان' (the fewer the same/likewise). This is elliptical: 'همان' is repeated for emphasis, and the two clauses contrast quantity while asserting sameness.
segments.s396-g56: 'چو' = 'as/like' introduces a simile; 'حلقه‌ست' describes something 'ring‑like' or 'an encircling band.' 'بر در' = 'on the door/gate.' 'بد بدگمان' is a doubled adjective phrase: 'bad' + 'bad‑goman' (mistrustful/suspicious), i.e. 'ill and suspicious.' The phrase compares the foregoing situation to a ring on the door of someone ill and distrustful.
segments.s396-g153: همه (addressing everyone) is the addressee; پیش یزدان is a prepositional phrase meaning "in the presence of / before God" (پیش + noun). نیایش کنید is a second-person plural imperative/subjunctive (= "pray/offer supplication"); the verb کند/کنید here means "perform/do" (i.e., perform the prayer). Word order is topical (همه) + location (پیش یزدان) + verb phrase (نیایش کنید).
segments.s396-g154: بخوانید is an imperative/optative plural meaning "recite" (read or chant prayers). و simply coordinates the two commands. او را uses the clitic را as a direct‑object marker; او refers back to God ("Him"). ستایش کنید is the matching plural imperative "praise/worship him."
segments.s396-g155: مگر here introduces an exception/conditional meaning "unless." کاین is fused classical form of "که این" (= "that these / these"), pointing to the following noun. بلاها is the plural "calamities/afflictions." ز ما بگذرد uses ز as an ablative/"from," and بگذرد is a present/subjunctive form meaning "pass away / be removed from us." The clause therefore says "unless these calamities pass from us."
segments.s396-g156: کز is a classical conjunction here (= "that / so that"); این پس literally "after this" = "henceforth / from now on." کسی is "anyone / no one" depending on context; مان is an old/poetic form (here an enclitic referring to "us/ourselves") that is not represented by the provided senses (so treated as an archaic pronoun). به کس نشمرد uses به as the prepositional/lexical marker "to/for," کس = "anyone / person," and نشمرد is the negative past/optative of شمردن "to count/consider," meaning "would not count/consider." The clause therefore means roughly "that from now on no one will reckon us (to anyone / as anything)."
segments.s396-g157: پشوتن is a proper name (the subject). بیامد is a past-tense verb "came." به پیش خدای is the prepositional phrase "to the presence of God" (به + پیش + خدای). Word order is subject + verb + prepositional destination.
segments.s396-g158: که introduces a relative clause "who/that." او refers back to the antecedent (here God) and بود is the past copula "was." بر نیکویی رهنمای uses بر with a noun of direction/purpose (here "toward goodness") and رهنمای is a predicate noun "guide / adviser." The phrase reads "who was a guide to goodness."
segments.s396-g159: نیایش (prayer) is the object; ز here is the ablative/marker of extent "to/from (a) measure," and اندازه means "measure/degree/extent." بگذاشتند is past plural "they left/abandoned / ceased." Together the idiom ز اندازه بگذاشتند expresses that they abandoned prayer to a great/excessive degree (i.e., they stopped praying).
segments.s396-g160: همه = "everyone / all." در زمان here means "at that time / at once" (temporal setting). دست برداشتند is a past‑tense plural idiom literally "they lifted/took away their hands," meaning "they withdrew their hands / desisted" (i.e., they stopped the action). The clause therefore says everyone withdrew their hands at that moment.
segments.s396-g129: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; 'سپیده' is the subject 'dawn' and 'از کوه' is the source ('from the mountain'). 'سر برکشید' is a classical verbal phrase in past tense (third-person singular) meaning 'raised/lifted its head' (سر = head, برکشید = lifted). Word order is subject + temporal conjunction + source + verbal phrase.
segments.s396-g130: 'شب' is the subject 'night'; 'آن چادر شعر' is a noun phrase 'that tent/veil of poetry' (چادر modified by شعر). The preposition-like 'در' here is used poetically to indicate placement/covering ('over/upon'), and 'سرکشید' (past, 3sg) is used poetically to mean 'drew up/put on' or 'raised (over itself)'. The whole clause describes night putting on its poetic veil.
segments.s396-g131: چو marks another temporal clause 'when'; 'خورشید تابان' is the compound subject 'the shining/radiant sun'. 'نهان کرد' is a two-word predicate where نهان (hidden) + کرد ('did/made') yields 'hid' — a common classical construction where کرد functions as the light verb.
segments.s396-g132: همی + past verb 'رفت' expresses iterative or continuous action in classical Persian ('kept going' / 'was going'). 'خون در پسِ پشت اوی' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'blood in his rear/behind him' — 'در' = in, 'پس پشت' = the rear/behind, and 'اوی' is an archaic third-person pronoun 'his/him'.
segments.s396-g133: 'به منزل رسید' is a verbal phrase meaning 'reached/arrived at the lodging or stage (of a journey)'; 'آن سپاه گران' is the subject noun phrase 'that heavy/weighty (i.e., formidable) army' with گران as an attributive adjective. Classical word order places the verbal phrase before the full subject phrase here.
segments.s396-g134: 'همه' quantifies the coordinated nouns that follow: 'گرزداران و نیزه‌وران' — 'mace-bearers and spearmen'. The nouns are plural/agentive compounds describing the types of fighters; the conjunction 'و' links them.
segments.s396-g135: 'بهاری' (spring-like) modifies 'روز' (day); 'یکی' here is an indefinite 'a certain' that precedes the noun phrase. 'خوش‌منش' is an adjective 'good-natured/delightful' attributive to 'روز'. The clause ends with the past copula 'بود' ('was'), giving a predicative description of the day.
segments.s396-g136: Two epithets are given in apposition/predication: 'دل‌افروز' ('heart-pleasing') and 'گیتی‌افروز' ('world-illuminating'), joined by 'یا' ('or') as stylistic alternatives; the final 'بود' is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s396-g89: شما is the 2nd-person plural/polite pronoun ('you'). بازگردید is a 2nd-person-plural imperative/polite-form verb 'return!' (classical/polite -ید ending). The adjectives پیروز و شاد follow the verb as predicate/adjectival complements describing the addressed group; there is no copula.
segments.s396-g90: مرا کام is a possessive/object construction meaning 'my desire' (مرا functioning like an older poss./object form before the noun). جز introduces an exception 'except/other than' and is followed by the verbal-noun phrase رزم جستن ('seeking/striving for battle'), where جستن is the infinitive/gerund used as a noun. مباد is an optative/negative wish (archaic/poetic) meaning 'may it not be', here used to express 'may I have no desire except ...' (so the whole clause: 'may my desire be nothing but the seeking of battle').
segments.s396-g91: به گفتار here means 'by/according to/under the influence of the speech/advice'; به functions as a preposition. این دیو is the demonstrative + noun 'this demon', and ناسازگار is a postpositive adjective 'discordant/hostile' modifying دیو (classical adjective-after-noun order).
segments.s396-g92: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the verb. سرکشیدید is a 2nd-person-plural past/perfective form of سرکشیدن ('to rise up, rebel'), here 'you rebelled/became insubordinate'. از کارزار is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of the battlefield' (از = 'from', followed by the noun کارزار).
segments.s396-g93: از ایران is a source phrase 'from Iran'. نخواهم is 1st-person-singular negative of خواستن ('I do not want / I will not ask for'). بر این رزم means 'for this battle' (بر used in the sense 'toward/for' + این رزم), and کس is 'anyone/any person'; word order is slightly marked for emphasis but the clause means 'I desire/ask for no one of Iran for this battle.'
segments.s396-g94: پسر با برادر is 'the son with (my) brother' (پسر is the person being assigned, با برادر = 'with brother'). م + را together form the older possessive/object 'مرا' ('my' / 'to me') followed by یار 'companion/ally' used predicatively. بس at the end is 'enough' / 'sufficient' (a final evaluative particle), so the clause means 'Let the son be with my brother as my companion — that suffices.'
segments.s396-g95: جهاندار ('world-holder'/sovereign) is the subject; پیروز is a postpositive adjective 'victorious'. یار is a predicative noun 'companion/ally', and the final منست is an archaic/polite copular/predicative element (here functioning like 'is mine / is with me' → 'is my ally'). The phrase says the sovereign, victorious, is (my) companion.
segments.s396-g96: سر اختر 'the chief/head star' is a nominal phrase (سر = 'chief/head' modifying اختر 'star'). اندر کنار is a locative expression 'at/at the side of' (اندر = 'in/at', کنار = 'side/beside'), and again منست is the archaic copulative/predicative element meaning 'is (at) / belongs to (me)'—so 'the chief star is at my side.'
segments.s396-g41: برانی here functions as a poetic adjective/predicate meaning “desolate / banished” characterizing the tract; it is followed by a locative-phrase introduced by بر + demonstrative (بر این‌گونه) meaning “in this manner.” فرسنگ چل is a unit phrase (فرسنگ = ‘farsang/parasang’) with چل following as a numeral modifier 'forty' (the adjective follows the unit in this older poetic order).
segments.s396-g42: نه ... و نه is the correlative negative pair “neither ... nor.” با is the preposition “with/by,” linking the means; اسپ تاو should be read as a named steed (a horse), and با + مرد دل means “by/with a man of heart” (a brave man). Word order is straightforward: preposition + instrument/person.
segments.s396-g43: وز آنجا is an archaic conjunctive/locative sequence meaning “and/from there” (وز = و + ز, classical), followed by به + place (به رویین‌دژ = “to/into the Rooyin‑dezh”). آید is the present/future third‑person of آمدن “to come,” and سپاه is the subject ‘army/host.’
segments.s396-g44: ببینی is the present/subjunctive (or future-oriented) form of دیدن with the verbal prefix بـ showing modality: here ‘you/one will see’ or ‘you will behold.’ یک modyfies the following adjective + noun (مایه‌ور جایگاه) to mean ‘a single/one place,’ and مایه‌ور is an adjective ‘wealthy / rich in (something)’ placed before جایگاه ‘place.’
segments.s396-g45: زمینش is the possessed noun (زمین + ـش = “its land/soil”). به کام is an idiom meaning “to the wish/for the satisfaction of,” followed by نیاز ‘need/necessity.’ اندر است (اندر + است) is the locative copular phrase ‘is within / lies in,’ so the whole clause says the land lies at the mercy/fulfilment of need (i.e. it readily supplies needs).
segments.s396-g46: وگر is the conditional ‘but if / however if.’ باره here means ‘load / burden / cargo’ (i.e. an occasion of goods). با + مه uses مه in its archaic/epic sense ‘great, mickle’ so با مه = ‘with great (measure).’ به راز اندر است is ‘is kept/kept within secrecy’ (به راز = in secret; اندر است = lies within). The clause thus says: if a load arrives, it is kept hidden with great secrecy.
segments.s396-g47: بشد is an optative/subjunctive form (may it be / let it be) used poetically. بامش is بام + ـش ‘its roof/summit.’ از introduces the standard comparative ‘than’ (از ابر ...), and بارنده‌تر is the comparative adjective ‘more rain‑bearing / more rain‑bringing,’ so the clause reads as a wish: ‘may its roof be more rain‑bearing than the cloud.’
segments.s396-g48: که introduces the explanatory/restrictive clause ‘for/that,’ بد is the adjective ‘bad/ill,’ نامش is نام + ـش ‘its name/reputation,’ and از ابر برنده‌تر is a comparative ‘sharper/more cutting than the cloud’ (برنده‌تر = more cutting/sharp). The clause gives a reason: its bad name cuts more sharply than the cloud (i.e. its ill fame is severe).
segments.s396-g169: This is a simple SOV clause: سپهبد (subject) + گرانمایگان را (definite/accusative object marked by را) + verb بخواند at the end. In this context بخواند is a past/performative verb meaning 'summoned/called' (an older poetic form of 'خواند' used here as 'he had them called').
segments.s396-g170: بسی داستانهای نیکو is a noun phrase ('many/much — stories good') followed by the verb براند. بسی here functions as an intensive/quantifying adverb ('many' / 'much'). The verb براند is clause-final; its exact nuance is poetic/archaic (contextually 'told/related' rather than literally 'drive'), so its semantic value is determined by the surrounding narration.
segments.s396-g171: چنین گفت begins the reported speech ('thus he said'). The following kایدر بمانید بار is an imperative/address: کایدر (vocative/adverb 'here' or 'O here') + بمانید (2nd/3rd‑person plural imperative or jussive 'remain/stay') and بار ('the baggage/load' — object). Word order places the imperative before or together with its object in poetic diction.
segments.s396-g172: This continues the reported command: مدارید is a negative or prohibitive instruction in this context (poetic verbal form meaning 'do not take away / do not move' rather than the literal senses listed), followed by جز آلت کارزار: جز 'except', آلت 'instrument/implement', کارزار 'battle/war'. The clause thus restricts what may be moved to only war implements.
segments.s396-g173: هرانکس که هستند is a common formula 'whoever (there) is' — هرانکس (indefinite 'whoever') + که (relative) + هستند (3rd‑pl. poetic/existential form used with the relative). سرهنگ‌فش is an appositive/title identifying the person ('the one bearing the colonel's standard / colonel‑clad').
segments.s396-g174: This is the result/provision clause: که باشد (subjunctive/wish or 'shall be/let there be') + ورا ('to him') + باره صد ('a burden/charge of a hundred') + آب‌کش ('water‑carrier' formed by آب + agentive کش). The order places the recipient (ورا) before the quantified burden.
segments.s396-g175: This clause lists supplies to be given: به ('to/for') + پنجاه (fifty) + آب (water) + و (and) + خورش (stew/food) followed by برنهید, a verb meaning 'set on/put upon; equip' in this context — an imperative or jussive instruction to supply these items.
segments.s396-g176: دگر (again/other) + آلت (implements) + گسترش (a poetic/technical word here; the exact sense is specialized or archaic) + بر (on/upon) + نهید (verb 'place/set' in poetic form). Together the clause directs that the remaining/other implements be put on them; نهید is the verb that closes the clause.
segments.s396-g177: فزونی ('surplus') is the noun subject or theme; هم ایدر ('also here') is an adverbial phrase (هم = 'also/too', ایدر = archaic poetic form of 'here') that modifies the clause. بمانید is a 3rd‑person plural subjunctive/jussive form of ماندن used optatively/prescriptively ('let ... remain'); بار ('load, baggage') names what is to be left. The word order is somewhat poetic (theme + locative + jussive verb + object) but reads as 'let the excess baggage remain here.'
segments.s396-g178: مگر is the exception conjunction 'except'; آنچه is the relative 'that which/whatever'; باید is the impersonal/modal 'must/ought' (present tense meaning 'must be taken/used'); ب + دان here forms a contracted locative/directional (poetic بدآن/بدان = 'to that') pointing to the destination; کارزار is 'battle(bfield)'. Together this ellipses the verb 'be taken' (implied) so the phrase means 'except whatever must be taken to that battle.'
segments.s396-g179: به نیروی یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'by the power of God' (به = 'by/with', نیروی = 'strength/power', یزدان = 'God'). بیابیم is 1st‑person plural subjunctive/optative ('may we find/obtain'), placed before its object; دست here means 'help/assistance' rather than the literal 'hand'. The whole segment is an optative wish: 'may we, by God's power, obtain help.'
segments.s396-g180: بدان is the contracted poetic form of به + آن ('to/against that' or 'to them') introducing the target; بدکنش is an attributive noun/adjective 'ill‑doing' or 'wicked' modifying مردم ('people'), and بت‌پرست specifies their idolatrous character. The phrase functions as the object/target of the preceding wish: 'against those ill‑doing idol‑worshipping people.'
segments.s396-g181: چو is the temporal/conditional 'when/if'; نومید functions predicatively as 'become hopeless/despairing'; گردد (from گشتن) is 'becomes' (present/future form used in narrative/conditional sense); ز یزدان indicates the source/object of despair ('about/of God'); کسی is the indefinite subject 'someone/anyone.' The clause reads 'when/if anyone becomes hopeless about God.'
segments.s396-g182: از او literally 'from him' (= 'for him'); نیک‌بختی 'good fortune'; نیاید is the negative present/subjunctive/impersonal 'does not come' (used here as future/resultative: 'will not come'); بسی is an adverb 'much' or 'greatly' placed after the verb for emphasis. Together: 'much good fortune will not come to him.'
segments.s396-g183: از آن دژ is a locative/origin phrase 'out of that fortress' (از = 'from/out of', آن = 'that', دژ = 'fortress'). یکایک is an adverb 'one by one' / 'each and every'; توانگر is an adjective/noun 'wealthy' and شوید (from شدن) is the 3rd‑person plural form 'they became' (classical narrative often uses such subjunctive/optative forms for past). The clause thus says 'each one became wealthy out of that fortress.'
segments.s396-g184: همه پاک uses همه 'all' and پاک adverbially ('completely/entirely' here) to intensify the result; با introduces accompaniment/means 'with'; گنج 'treasure' and افسر 'helmet' (here meaning helmets/equipment) are the possessions/equipment; شوید again is 'they became' / 'were'—so the line means 'all were completely (i.e., fully) provided with treasure and helmets.'
segments.s396-g97: به مردی places the target/goal with the preposition به ('to/for'); نباید is an impersonal modal 'must not/should not' that governs an omitted finite clause. کسی is the indefinite subject (here functioning as 'no one' in negative scope), and همرهم (poetic form of همره) is a predicate noun 'companion' — word order is inverted for verse (object/phrase before the verb/modal), so literally 'to a man should-not anyone [be] companion'.
segments.s396-g98: اگر ... وگر introduces two alternative protases: 'if ... and/or if ...'. ستانم is a first-person present/subjunctive verb meaning 'I take/deprive' (here 'take life' = kill); دهم (present later in the segment though not listed in the provided word array) is the parallel verb 'I give' (i.e. 'spare or give life'). جان is the object 'life'. The pairing اگر ... وگر signals 'whether I do X or Y'.
segments.s396-g99: به دشمن is the indirect object 'to the enemy'; نمایم is first-person present/subjunctive used here as a declarative/future 'I will show'; هنر is the direct object 'skill/arts', modified by the following relative-like expression هرچه هست 'whatever there is' (هرچه 'whatever' + هست 'is'). Word order places the verb before the object for poetic effect but syntactically is 'I will show the enemy (the) skill, whatever exists.'
segments.s396-g100: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from / stemming from' that indicates source/origin of the 'هنر' just mentioned. A coordinated list follows: مردی (here 'manliness' or valor), پیروزی ('victory'), و زور دست ('and the strength/power of the hand') — together they specify the sources or kinds of skill. The nouns are simply coordinated by و; دِست is literal 'hand' (instrumental image for force).
segments.s396-g101: بیابید is a second-person plural imperative/subjunctive 'find / obtain' (verb-first imperative typical in poetry). هم adds 'also/even', and بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly' modifying the following noun آگهی 'information/notice' (object). The phrase reads as an instruction: 'Also find, without doubt, information.'
segments.s396-g102: از introduces a prepositional complement to آگهی: 'about/from/concerning this ...'. این 'this' points to what follows (the phrase نامور فر شاهنشهی). نامور qualifies فر ('renowned' modifies 'farr' — the royal splendour), and شاهنشهی is an adjectival/genitive noun 'of kingship/imperial' specifying the type of فر. Together: 'information about this famed royal farr of kingship.'
segments.s396-g103: که begins a relative clause ('which/that') attached to the preceding 'آگهی'. با دژ can be read as 'against/to the fortress' (با + noun often indicates instrument or involvement with the fortress), and چه کردم is the internal interrogative/expostulatory 'what I did' (چه 'what/how' + کردم 'I did'). به دستان و زور is an instrumental phrase 'by/with hands and strength' indicating means. The clause therefore reports 'what I did to/against the fortress by hand and force.'
segments.s396-g104: به نام is the customary formula 'in the name of' (به + نام), followed by a string of divine/celestial names. خداوند here functions as a title/epithet ('lord/God'), and کیوان and هور are proper names/astrological figures invoked thereafter — a conventional poetic invocation. The whole phrase reads 'in the name of Khoda(vand) Kiwan and Hur.'
segments.s396-g1: از آن پس is a temporal prepositional phrase: از (from) + آن (that/then) + پس (after), meaning “from that time on.” بفرمود is a polite/honorific past verb meaning “he commanded/said,” and تا introduces a subordinate clause here, functioning like English “that” or “so that.” گرگسار is the noun linked as the subject of the subordinate clause that follows (it will be realized by the verb in the next segment). Note classical word order: the main verb of the subordinate clause appears later.
segments.s396-g2: بیامد is a simple past/punctual verb “came” used to realize the action required by تا; Persian often places the verb after its arguments, but segmentation here shows it up front. بر is a preposition meaning “to/toward/before” (here “before” or “to the presence of”), and نامور شهریار is a noun phrase ‘the famed king’ with adjective (نامور) preceding the head (شهریار).
segments.s396-g3: بدادش is a past-form verb phrase with clitic object: (ب)داد + -ش = “he gave him/it to him” (classical contracted form). سه جام is a quantified noun phrase “three cups,” and دمادم is an adverb meaning “continually/ever-flowing,” modifying نبید (wine). Word order is typical Persian: verb (gave) followed by the object phrase, then modifiers.
segments.s396-g4: می‌سرخ is a present-tense/descriptive verbal form “it reddens/it blushed” (poetic present used to describe the wine); و links clauses. جام here resumes the object (‘a cup’) and از marks origin/material “from/of,” so گل شنبلید is a noun phrase ‘the flower (گل) of شنبلید’ (compound name), interpreted as the material/source of that cup. Persian allows noun + noun compounds and uses از for “of/from” relationships.
segments.s396-g5: بدو گفت is the classical sequence meaning “he said to him”: the clitic/indirect object بدو (“to him/unto it”) followed by گفت (said). کای is a vocative particle “O,” used to call someone. بد تن بدنهان is a stacked epithet: بد (bad/ill) + تن (body) + بدنهان (an intensive or variant of ‘body’), forming a disparaging address “ill-bodied one.” Adjectival epithets precede the noun in such classical usage.
segments.s396-g6: نگه کن is an imperative construction: نگه (look/see/gaze) + کن (do—imperative ‘do/look’). Here نگه is a noun/verb root used as an imperative phrase. ب + دین is written as two parts in the segmentation but functions together as the demonstrative/locative meaning “to/at this” (classical compound), so بدین کردگار جهان = “at this Maker of the World” (کردگار جهان = the Creator of the world). The clause is an imperative address telling the addressee to look at the Creator.
segments.s396-g7: نه ... نه ... is correlative negation “neither ... nor ...”; here نه precedes the first noun and is repeated to continue denial. سیمرغ پیدا = “a Simurgh was found/seen” uses پیدا as the predicate ‘visible/found,’ then the phrase continues with نه شیر و نه گرگ to add further items denied. Word order places the predicate (پیدا) immediately after the first noun in this poetic clause.
segments.s396-g8: This segment continues the negative list with نه followed by a complex noun phrase: آن (that) + تیز چنگ (sharp-clawed; تیز modifies چنگ) + اژدهای بزرگ (great dragon). Persian stacks modifiers before the head noun and allows noun + noun compounds (تیز چنگ = ‘sharp-clawed’). The whole phrase is denied as another absent creature.
segments.s396-g9: به منزل is a locative prepositional phrase 'at/to the halt/stage'; که introduces a relative/clausal link 'that/when' and is followed by the verb انگیزد (3rd sg. present/presentive of انگیختن) 'stirs/arouses'. Poetic word order places the verb before its explicit subject این بار شور ('this time/occasion of turmoil'); بار here means 'occasion/time' and شور is 'passion, tumult'.
segments.s396-g10: بود is the past/existential 'there was'; it introduces the inventory that follows. آب و جای گیای ستور lists what existed: آب 'water' and جای 'place' modified by گیای (an adjective 'leafy/green') describing ستور 'steeds/horses' (i.e. grassy pasture for horses).
segments.s396-g11: به آواز is an adverbial phrase 'in a voice / aloud' (به + noun used adverbially). گفت here functions as the speech verb 'said' (past), and آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that moment'. گرگسار is an epithet/nickname applied to the speaker — literally 'wolf‑ish' or 'ferocious' — used as the subject.
segments.s396-g12: که‌ای here functions as an exclamatory/vocative particle (poetic combination of که + ای) introducing a direct address 'O...'. نامور and فرخ are epithets placed before the proper name (both attributive): نامور 'renowned' and فرخ 'fortunate/blessed', followed by the name اسفندیار.
segments.s396-g13: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. باز گردی = 'you return/turn back' (poetic/older verb form). نباشد شگفت is an impersonal negative construction meaning 'there will be no wonder' or 'it would not be surprising' (شگفت as noun 'wonder'). The clause as a whole says 'If you return, it will be no surprise.'
segments.s396-g14: ز بخت تو 'from your fate/fortune' (ز = from, بخت = fate/fortune, تو = your). اندازه باید گرفت is an idiomatic statement 'one must take/accept a measure/limit' (اندازه = degree/extent, باید = must/it is necessary, گرفت = take). Together: 'One must accept the limits set by your fate.'
segments.s396-g15: ترا is the clitic form meaning 'to you' (ت + را combined as the dative/accusative). یار بود literally 'was/ be a friend/ally' — here past tense can carry an optative/blessing sense 'may ... be your ally'. ایزد‑ای is a vocative 'O God' (ای added for address) and نیکبخت is an epithet 'fortunate/blessed'. The phrase functions as a short blessing: 'May God the fortunate be your companion.'
segments.s396-g16: به بار آمد is an idiom 'to come into bearing / to bear fruit' (به + بار + آمد). آن is the demonstrative 'that', خسروانی is an adjective 'royal' modifying درخت 'tree'. Literally: 'that royal tree came to bear [fruit]' — a poetic way to say the royal stock produced fruit/results.
segments.s396-g161: همانگه is a temporal adverb meaning 'at that moment' placed before the verb for emphasis. بیامد is a 3rd-person singular past tense 'came'. یکی باد خوش is an indefinite noun phrase ('a pleasant/a certain wind') where یکی ('a certain') precedes the noun in poetic/inverted word order (subject comes after the verb).
segments.s396-g162: ببرد here functions as a past action 'carried/took away' (3rd-person singular); poetic narrative sometimes uses forms that look like present/subjunctive but are past in context. ابر is the object 'cloud'. و is the coordinating 'and'. روی هوا is a locative phrase 'on/upon the air/sky' (روی = 'upon'). گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned'. کش is a terse poetic adjective/qualifier ('long/wide' in this context) attached after گشت to mean 'became broad/extended' rather than its literal senses listed, so no direct match in the provided sense list.
segments.s396-g163: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning 'when'. ایرانیان را uses را as a topic/definiteness marker marking whose hearts we speak of ('the Iranians' — object/topic). دل آمد is an idiomatic predicate: literally 'the heart came', meaning 'their hearts were settled/they felt at ease'; آمد is used in the sense 'happened/wasfalling into place' rather than physical 'come'.
segments.s396-g164: ببودند is the 3rd-person plural past of بودن 'they were/they stood/they remained'. بر پیش یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of God' (بر پیش = 'before/in front of'), with یزدان 'God/divine power' as the object of the preposition. به پای is a locution 'at the feet' (literally 'to/at foot'), so the whole segment means they stood/assembled before God at his feet.
segments.s396-g165: سراپرده و خیمه‌ها is a compound subject 'inner chambers and the tents' (خیمه‌ها plural). گشته‌تر is a past participle گشته ('become/turned') with the comparative suffix -تر 'more', so together 'had become even more…' — a poetic predicate placed after the subject.
segments.s396-g166: ز سرما is an ablative/causal phrase 'because of/from the cold'. کسی را نبد is an idiomatic negative-existential: نبد (archaic negative of بودن) 'there was not', with کسی را ('anyone/no one') as the logical patient; the construction yields 'there was no one'. پای و پر is a terse, somewhat elliptical coordination 'foot and feather' (literal items listed to stress absence of people/movement or garments); grammatically it follows the negative predicate as the items that are absent or not seen.
segments.s396-g167: همانجا is a locative adverb 'there'. ببودند is past plural 'they were/they stayed'. گردان is the subject 'troop/company' and سه روز is a temporal duration 'for three days' following the subject; the verb is placed between adverb and subject in this poetic word order.
segments.s396-g168: چهارم is an ordinal ('on the fourth'); چو is the conjunction 'when'. بفروخت here is a poetically archaic verb form: in this context it functions as a pictorial verb meaning 'burst forth/shone' (not the ordinary sense 'sold'), used with گیتی فروز 'world-illuminator' (an epithet for the sun) as its subject. The clause thus means 'when the world-illuminator shone forth'.
segments.s396-g137: سراپرده و خیمه is a topical/vocative phrase (“the inner-chamber and the tents”); فرمود is the reporting verb “he said/asked”; کی is the interrogative “who” following the verb — a common poetic order meaning “he asked, ‘who…?’.” Together it frames the following clause as his question to those in the tents.
segments.s396-g138: This clause contains two coordinated verb phrases. بیاراست is a perfective/preterite-form meaning “(has) set/arranged” (literally “adorned”) and خوان is the direct object “feast/table.” و links the verbs; بیاورد here functions as a perfective/past “brought” or an older-form third-person verb “brings/brung,” and می is the object “wine.” The sequence answers the question introduced before: “who has set the feast and brought wine?”
segments.s396-g139: هم‌اندر زمان is an adverbial temporal phrase “at the same time / meanwhile.” تندبادی ز کوه is a noun phrase “a gale/strong wind from the mountain,” where ز is the poetic preposition “from.” Word order places the temporal phrase first for emphasis.
segments.s396-g140: برآمد is the main verb “arose/issued forth.” که introduces a relative/explanatory clause (“which/so that”); شد نامور is “became renowned/famous.” زان ستوه is a prepositional phrase “from/for that weariness/distress” giving the cause or consequence — overall: “a wind rose which became notorious because of that hardship.”
segments.s396-g141: جهان سربسر گشت is an idiomatic predication: جهان “the world” + سربسر گشت “became entirely/throughout.” چون introduces a simile “like,” and پر زاغ is a genitive phrase “a (crow’s) feather.” The order is typical poetic word-order: topic + predicate + simile.
segments.s396-g142: This is a negational/perceptual clause. ندانست is the past negative of دانستن “did not/was unable to know/distinguish.” کس here functions as “no one” (negative polarity with ندانست). باز هامون is a compressed noun phrase = بازِ هامون “the hawk of the plain” (the ezāfe is omitted in poetry); ز introduces the comparison “from,” and زاغ is “crow.” Literally: “No one knew (could distinguish) the plain’s hawk from the crow.”
segments.s396-g143: In this poetic clause بیارید is an older/poetic verb form meaning “they brought” or an imperative/optative in context — here best read as a past action “brought.” از ابر تاریک is the source phrase “from the dark cloud(s),” and برف is the direct object “snow.”
segments.s396-g144: زمینی is an adjectival/locative modifier “of the land / earthly”; پر از is the fixed phrase “full of,” so پر از برف = “full of snow.” و links the next noun phrase بادی شگرف, where بادی (poetically used as ‘a wind’) is modified post‑nominally by the adjective شگرف “wondrous/marvelous.” The whole phrase describes the scene: “a land full of snow and a marvelous wind.”
segments.s396-g121: نگردد is a jussive/subjunctive-form verb (verb before its object, common in classical Persian) meaning 'may ... not happen' or 'let ... not come to pass'. فرامش is the abstract noun 'forgetfulness' as the thing that must not 'become'. به دل رنجتان is a prepositional phrase 'into/into the heart of your suffering' (به + دل + plural/possessive رنجتان), giving the direction/target of فرامش.
segments.s396-g122: نماند is a past-tense-root form used here with negative sense 'will not remain/ be left' (verb first). تهی is the predicate 'empty' following the verb. بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly' modifying the clause, and گنجتان ('your treasure') is the possessed noun that is the semantic subject/affected item placed finally for emphasis.
segments.s396-g123: همی is an adverb indicating repeated or continuous action ('continually/kept on'). رای زد is the verbal phrase 'to take/strike a counsel/plan' (رای = counsel/plan, زد = did/struck), with زد following رای (object-like noun + simple verb). تا introduces the temporal clause 'until', جهان شد خنک = 'the world became cool' where شد is the copular/become verb and خنک is the predicate adjective.
segments.s396-g124: The clause has the verb برفت (an older past form meaning 'went forth/rose'), followed by the source phrase از بر کوه 'from over the mountain' (از + بر + کوه), and the subject noun باد سبک 'a light wind' (باد = wind, سبک = light). Word order is verb first (poetic), then source, then the nominal subject; in prose this would be باد سبک از بر کوه برفت.
segments.s396-g125: برآمد is a past verb meaning 'arose/issued forth' (verb initial). ز درگاه is a source phrase 'from the portal/threshold' (ز = from, درگاه = gate/royal portal). شیپور و نای are the instruments that issued forth (two nouns joined by و 'and'): 'trumpet and ney (reed‑flute)'.
segments.s396-g126: سپه (the army/host) is the noun; برگرفتند is a past plural verb 'they took up/lifted/rose' (here 'rose up' or 'set out' intransitively of the host). یکسر is an adverb 'at once/entirely' modifying برگرفتند; ز جای is a source phrase 'from their places' (ز = from, جای = place). Word order places verb before its noun-subject for poetic effect.
segments.s396-g127: به کردار آتش is a simile/adverbial phrase 'in the manner of fire' (به = in/by, کردار = manner/conduct, آتش = fire). همی again marks continuous or vigorous action ('continually/with force'). راندند is the past plural verb 'they drove' (transitive; here applied to horses or impetus), so the clause means 'they drove on like fire.'
segments.s396-g128: جهان‌آفرین is the direct object 'the world‑creator / Creator' and را is the object marker placing emphasis on that object. بسی here functions adverbially 'many times / repeatedly' (classical usage), and خواندند is the past plural verb 'they called/invoked/uttered (a call or prayer)'. The phrase thus means 'they repeatedly called on the Creator.'
segments.s396-g105: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. ایرانیان is the subject ('the Iranians'). برگشادند is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural verb 'they opened' (poetic word order places the verb before its object). چشم is the object 'eyes' (here placed after the verb rather than before).
segments.s396-g106: بدیدند is the past 3rd-person plural 'they saw'. چهر is the noun 'face' (direct object). ورا is the enclitic object pronoun (classical form of 'او را'), here referring to 'him'/'his face'. پر ز is a fixed poetic phrase 'full of' (پر + ز), and خشم is the noun 'anger' giving the content of fullness. Word order follows Persian: object(s) before the verb in some constructions but here the verb begins the clause and objects follow for emphasis.
segments.s396-g107: برفتند is past 3rd-person plural 'they went/proceeded'. پوزش‌کنان is an adverbial participle (converb) formed with -ان meaning 'apologetically' or 'making excuses'. نزد شاه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to the presence of the king' ('نزد' = 'near/in the presence of').
segments.s396-g108: که‌گر is a fused conditional particle (که + گر) meaning 'that if' / 'if'. شاه is the subject of the conditional clause. بیند is the 3rd-person singular present (classical usage here; can function as a subjunctive/optative) 'sees'. ببخشد is the 3rd-singular present-form of بخشیدن used optatively/subjunctively 'may/would forgive'. گناه is the object 'sin' or 'guilt'. The clause is an indirect plea: 'if the king sees (it/them), he may forgive the sin.'
segments.s396-g109: فدای تو is a formulaic predication 'sacrificed for you' (فدای used as 'sacrifice for'). بادا is an optative particle 'may it be' (archaic/poetic). تن و جان ما lists the possessed nouns 'our body and life/soul' with the possessive pronoun ما placed after the coordinated nouns.
segments.s396-g110: بر این بود is a past-tense clause 'this was (the case)' with بر + demonstrative این marking 'concerning/with respect to this'. تا بود here means 'as long as' / 'while' (تا + past بود = 'while it was/existed'). پیمان ما 'our pact/covenant' is the noun plus possessive; word order is noun then pronoun. Together: 'this was our position/purpose as long as our covenant lasted.'
segments.s396-g111: ز بهر is a poetic double prepositional phrase 'for the sake of' or 'on account of' (ز + بهر). تن شاه is a genitive-like sequence 'the king's body/person' (noun followed by the possessor). غمخواره‌ایم is the present 1st-person-plural copular construction 'we are mourners/are sorrowful' (غمخواره + ایم = 'we grieve').
segments.s396-g112: نه negates the clause ('not'). از marks cause/agency 'because of' (here 'not because of'). کوشش و جنگ list the causes 'effort (toil) and war'. بیچاره‌ایم is the present 1st-plural 'we are unfortunate/wretched'. Together the clause says 'it is not because of toil and war that we are wretched.'
segments.s396-g73: This clause is a protasis (conditional) formed without an explicit اگر. به راهی دگرگر شوی uses the verb شوی (2nd/3rd-sg. subjunctive/present of شدن) to mean “become / turn (to).” به راهی (preposition + way) marks direction; دگرگر is an adjective “other/different.” The sequence is poetic: prepositional phrase + adjective + subjunctive verb, giving the sense “if you turn to another path/become other.”
segments.s396-g74: همه شهر توران is the subject phrase “all the towns of Turan” (city noun follows quantifier). برندت is a verb (برند + enclitic ـت) where ـت is the 2nd-person object clitic “you”; in older/poetic Persian the verb can stand before an adjunct like نماز, so برندت نماز reads “they will take/carry you (برندت) to prayer (نماز).” The verb form is a present/hortative or apodotic form describing the consequence of the condition.
segments.s396-g75: بدین سان is a fixed phrase meaning “in this way / thus” (بدین = ب + دین in poetic splitting). که introduces a reported clause; گوید همی is a stative/ habitual present (همی = continuous particle) meaning “keeps saying” or “is always saying.” گرگسار is an epithet applied to the speaker (“the wolfish one”); word order here places the manner phrase and report-marker before the quoted content.
segments.s396-g76: تن خویش را = “your body” (تن = body; خویش is the possessive made of خوی + enclitic ش; را is the definite/direct-object marker). خوارمایه مدار is an admonition: خوارمایه = “despicable/worthless” and مدار is a negative imperative meaning “do not make / do not treat as.” The clause thus gives the prohibition with object marked by را.
segments.s396-g77: از آن پس که = a temporal/concessive phrase “from/after that, since.” پیروز گشتیم is past tense plural (گشتیم = we became) meaning “we became victorious.” The conjunction و links the predicate شاد (“happy/joyful”) to the preceding clause; the whole segment sets the temporal/circumstantial background.
segments.s396-g78: نباید is an impersonal modal “one must not / it should not” followed by the infinitive دادن (“to give/inflict”). سر خویش را = “one’s own head” with خویش + enclitic ش and object marker را. به باد دادن (literally “to give to the wind”) is an idiom meaning to throw away or squander (here: to be reckless with one’s life/head). باد is literal “wind.”
segments.s396-g79: چو = “when.” بشنید is a past 3rd-person (or perfect) “he heard”; این‌گونه ز ایشان سخن = “such speech from them” (ز = poetic preposition ‘from’). The object phrase (این‌گونه ز ایشان سخن) comes before the verb, a normal order in poetry, so the segment reads “when he heard such words from them.”
segments.s396-g80: شد is the verb “became / it came about” used here to mark a change of state. آن تازه رویش = “that fresh-faced one” (تازه = fresh/new; روی + ـش = his/her face/appearance, used as an epithet). ز گردان کهن = “from the old troop/host” with ز indicating separation or source. Together the clause expresses that the fresh-faced man changed his relation to the old band (i.e., left or separated from them).
segments.s396-g145: سه روز و سه شب is a paired time expression ('three days and three nights') placed first as a temporal adverbial. هم بدان سان is an adverbial phrase meaning 'also/in that same way' (بدان سان = be- + دان + سان, a fixed poetic phrase 'in that manner'), and به دشت is a locative prepositional phrase 'to/over the plain' giving the place for the following verb in the next segment.
segments.s396-g146: دم باد is a genitive noun phrase 'the breath/gust of the wind' (دم = breath/gust + باد = wind). ز اندازه اندر گذشت is a poetic predication meaning 'passed beyond measure'; ز here marks 'from/out of' (used idiomatically), اندازه = 'degree/extent', اندر گذشت (with گذشت in past tense) = 'passed' or 'surpassed'.
segments.s396-g147: هوا پود گشت is a metaphorical predication: هوا (the air/atmosphere) + پود (weft/fabric) + گشت (past 'became') — 'the air became like cloth.' ابر چون تار شد is a parallel clause: ابر (clouds) + چون تار ('like warp' — a simile) + شد ('became'). گشت/شد are past-state verbs forming the predicates.
segments.s396-g148: سپهبد is the subject (the spahbed, a military title). از آن کار introduces the cause ('because of that affair/event') with از used causally, and بیچار شد is the predicate 'became wretched' (بیچار = miserable; شد = became). Word order is Subject — causal phrase — predicate.
segments.s396-g149: به آواز is an adverbial phrase 'in a voice' = 'aloud'; پیش پشوتن is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of Peshotan' (پیش = before/in the presence of + proper name); بگفت is the past verb 'he said' (ب- prefix + گفت). The whole segment sets up the reported speech that follows.
segments.s396-g150: که introduces the reported clause 'that...'. این کار ما is 'this affair of ours' (ما is possessive/agent here). گشت is the past 'became/turned into', and با درد جفت is a predicative phrase 'paired with pain' (با = with, درد = sorrow/pain, جفت = pair). The clause means the affair has become matched with/added to their suffering.
segments.s396-g151: به مردی شدم is a change-of-state construction 'I became (or was reduced) to a man' (به marks the resulting state, شدم = I became). در دم اژدها is a locative/ablative phrase 'in the dragon's breath' (در = in, دم = breath/moment, اژدها = dragon) specifying the circumstance.
segments.s396-g152: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. زور کردن is a verbal noun phrase 'to use/exert strength' (زور = strength, کردن = light verb 'to do/use'). نیارد is an archaic/poetic negative/result verb here meaning 'does not bring / will not yield' (the exact dictionary entry given doesn't match perfectly, so sense is irregular/archaic), and بها is the object 'value/benefit' — together: 'now exerting strength will bring no benefit.'
segments.s396-g25: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). بازگردی is the present-subjunctive form meaning 'you return' (classical Persian uses the subjunctive for conditional propositions). نباشد follows the protasis and here functions as the predicate of the clause ('there will not be'); word order is inverted compared with modern Persian (predicate before noun). شگفت is a noun meaning 'wonder/surprise' and is what 'نباشد' negates.
segments.s396-g26: ز گفتار من introduces the reason/source phrase 'from my speech' (ز = poetic 'from'; گفتار = 'speech'; من = possessive 'my' here). کین نباید گرفت is an impersonal/modal construction: کین 'vengeance' is the object/topic and نباید گرفت literally 'should not be taken' (نباید as impersonal 'one should not', گرفت as 'take/seize'), so: 'one should not take vengeance.'
segments.s396-g27: همی is an archaic/aspectual particle often marking ongoing or emphatic action in epic style; ویژه here is adjectival/adverbial 'special/especially.' در خون لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'in/among the army's blood' (خون = 'blood' meaning 'bloodshed'). شوی is second-person singular subjunctive/present (used as future/characteristic) 'you will be / you become.'
segments.s396-g28: به + nouns here makes an adverbial manner phrase ('with ...' / '-ly'): به تندی و بدرایی و بدخوی = 'with swiftness and [b-] and ill temper.' The three nouns are linked by و as a series describing the manner of action; بدرایی is an archaic/poetic noun (sense not in the supplied options) and likely denotes a kind of boldness/ferocity, while بدخوی means 'ill-tempered.'
segments.s396-g29: مرا این درستست is the main clause with a dative/topic 'مرا' ('to me' / 'for me') plus the copular predicate 'این درستست' = 'this is correct/true.' کز introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'from which') and here introduces the content: 'that from a severe wind...' باد سخت is the subject of the following verb ('a strong/severe wind'). Note: in the text مـ + را are shown separately but together function as مرا (dative/object marking).
segments.s396-g30: بریزد is a present-subjunctive/optative form used here to express an event ('will pour' / 'will scatter') governed by the preceding 'that' clause. بر آن مرز is a locative phrase 'upon that border' (بر = 'on/upon'; آن modifies مرز). بار درخت is a genitive phrase meaning 'the burden/load of the tree' (بار = 'load/fruit/produce'; درخت = 'tree'), the thing that the wind will 'pour' or scatter.
segments.s396-g31: از آن پس که is a temporal connector 'after that, when' introducing a time-clause. اندر بیابان 'in the desert' is the locative phrase, and رسی is the present/subjunctive/second-person form 'you arrive' or more generally 'one arrives/reaches' — here used to mean 'when (one) reaches the desert.'
segments.s396-g32: یکی منزل آید is an independent clause 'one/a certain station comes' — یکی functions as 'a certain/one' and منزل here means 'a stage/stopping-place of the journey.' آید is the verb 'comes/is reached.' به فرسنگ سی is a measure phrase 'at/after thirty farsangs' (به introduces the measure and فرسنگ 'farsang' is the unit; the numeral follows in the manuscript).
segments.s396-g193: 'که گفتی' is a clause-introducing phrase meaning 'that you said' (که as the conjunction 'that' + گفتی 'you said' in past). 'بدین منزلت' is an archaic/poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'in/at this station/place' (here written as ب + دین in the segmentation but functioning together as 'بدین' = 'به + این'). The predicate 'آب نیست' ('there is no water') comes at the end: آب (water) + نیست (is not).
segments.s396-g194: همان is a demonstrative emphasising 'that/this very'. جای آرامش و خواب is a noun phrase 'a place of rest and sleep' (جای + آرامش + و + خواب). The sentence ends with نیست, the negative copula 'is not', so the phrase means 'it is not a place for repose or sleep.'
segments.s396-g195: کنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. ز آسمان is a prepositional phrase 'from the sky/heaven'. خاست is the literary past 'arose/began' (poetic past of خاستن). بانگ کلنگ is the noun phrase 'the sound/cry of the pickaxe' (بانگ governing کلنگ); the verb 'خاست' occurs before the nominative phrase for poetic word order.
segments.s396-g196: This is an address/question: دل ما ('our heart/our hearts') is the object of emotion; چرا کردی is an interrogative phrase 'why did you (do/make)' with کردی the 2nd-person past 'you made/did'. از آب تنگ is a causal/ablative phrase 'because of/from the narrow/scant water' (از + آب تنگ), giving the reason for the heart's distress.
segments.s396-g197: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. داد is the past tense verb 'gave' (here 'gave an answer'). پاسخ is the direct object 'answer/reply'. کز is the fused/archaic form meaning 'that from' or 'that/of which' (a contraction of که + از) introducing the subordinate clause. ایدر is an archaic locative 'here'; ستور is the noun 'beasts/animals' (the subject of the subordinate clause).
segments.s396-g198: نیابد is a literary negative-present/optative 3rd-person form of 'یافتن' meaning 'does not find / will not find' (here 'will find nothing'). مگر is the exception particle 'except'. چشمه آب شور is the noun phrase 'a spring of salty/brackish water' (چشمه + آب + شور), with شور used in the sense 'saltiness/brine'.
segments.s396-g199: دگر means 'another' or 'further/again' here introducing an alternative. چشمه آب‌یابی is a compound-like phrase 'the spring (or the act/person) of water-finding' (چشمه + آب‌یابی) meaning a source found/searched for by a water-diviner. چو is the simile 'like/as', and زهر is 'poison' used metaphorically ('like poison').
segments.s396-g200: کزان is a compound/archaic relative 'from which' (ک + زان), introducing origin. آب مرغ و ددان forms a beneficiary/genitive sequence 'water for the birds and (the) beasts' (مرغ = bird; ددان here is a poetic/variant form meaning beasts/animals). راست بهر is an adverbial phrase 'direct/straight for (their) benefit' (راست = straight/direct, بهر = for/toward). The whole segment says that from that spring the water goes straight for birds and beasts.
segments.s396-g201: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' placed before the verb for emphasis. گفت is the verb 'said' (here used with V S word order typical of epic Persian: 'چنین گفت سالار' = 'thus said the commander'). کز is the poetic contraction of 'که از' (= 'that/from') introducing the following ablative phrase; گرگسار is the noun 'wolf-haunted place' governed by کز.
segments.s396-g202: یکی here = 'a certain/one' introducing an indefinite person. راهبر is the noun 'guide/leader' and is the direct object of ساختم. ساختم is first-person past 'I made/appointed' — this is the speaker's (the سالار's) reported first-person statement. کینه‌دار follows as an appositive/adjectival phrase 'resentful' or 'holding hatred', describing that appointed guide.
segments.s396-g203: ز گفتار او is an ablative/causal phrase 'from/by his speech' (ز = poetic 'from/by'). تیز is an adjective 'swift' placed before لشکر in poetic inversion (Adj N rather than the more usual N Adj). براند is the third-person singular past of راندن 'to drive/lead' (here 'put in motion' or 'drove on'), agreeing with the implied agent in the narrative.
segments.s396-g204: جهاندار is a title 'sovereign' or 'world-ruler' and begins an object phrase. نیکی دهش functions as a compound epithet 'giver of goodness' (نیکی = 'goodness'; دهش here conveys the act/giving sense, forming 'one who gives good'). را is the definite object marker, and بخواند is a past third-person verb 'he called/summoned' (here 'he summoned [the sovereign, the giver of good]').
